text: Think process, not product. By open-sourcing the daily mess of your studio or office, you turn your creative journey into an ongoing narrative that people can genuinely connect with.
Share something small every single day. Don't worry about your output being perfect. Just look at your work-in-progress, apply the 'So What?' test, and put a fragment out there.
Don't turn into human spam. If you want fans, you have to be a fan first. You have to deeply care about what others are making if you expect them to give a damn about what you make.
Learn to take a punch. Your creative work is simply what you do, it is not who you are. Roll with the criticism, protect your mental space, and keep making things.
If you find a vampire in your life, drop them immediately. Life is far too short to spend your finite energy on people who drain your vitality and don't support your growth.
The romantic myth of the lone genius is dead. Exceptional breakthroughs are born from a 'scenius'—a decentralized, collaborative ecosystem of people who feed and inspire one another.
You don't have to be an undisputed expert to contribute massive value. You simply need to be an amateur with a passion for learning, open-sourcing your ongoing education in real time.
Open up your cabinet of curiosities. Do not hoard your inspirations out of a defensive fear of appearing unoriginal. Explicitly credit your masters and share your reading lists.
Teach what you know. The minute you learn something new, turn around and generously share it with others. Giving away your secrets doesn't diminish your value; it cements your authority.
there is zero shame in passing the hat and charging for your value. Money is simply the essential fuel that keeps your creative space open and your experiments funded.
Never let the pressure for money dictate the ultimate direction of your curiosity. Collect coin to fund your freedom, not to water down the bold extremes of your work.
To survive decades of creative exposure, you must learn to stick around. Build your lifestyle entirely around the boundary of a single day, practicing the daily dispatch.
When a topic or craft no longer brings you psychological vitality, you must have the courage to destroy your secure foundations, assume the beginner's mind, and reinvent yourself.
When I have the privilege of talking to my readers, the most common questions they ask me are about self-promotion. How do I get my stuff out there? How do I get noticed? How do I find an audience?
Creativity is not a talent. It is a way of operating. To build a resonant audience, you must move away from guarding your secrets to treating your path as an open, public artifact.
The pressure to accumulate a massive body of work or complete a final masterpiece causes intense psychological stalling and creative paralysis. Focus entirely on today.
You are under no obligation to provide a platform for bad-faith criticism. True creative safety requires you to ruthlessly block trolls and protect your internal peace.
Your website is your ultimate digital hub—a self-hosted space that you own completely, entirely free from the volatile swings of social media algorithms.
By documenting and sharing your daily drift, you invite an audience into an authentic story. This transparency strips away the intimidating aura of expertise.
Self-promotion fails when it is driven by ego, but it flourishes beautifully when it is approached as an act of service, teaching what you know to the scene.
